coredata.dc:title: Distinguishable photocatalytic activity of nano polyaniline with quantum dots metal oxide as photocatalysts for photodegradation of Dianix blue dye and different industrial pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Abu-Melha, Sraa
coredata.dc:description: Nanopolyaniline (PAN) polymers have recently been under active investigation as promising photocatalysts. In this work, a simple modified sol–gel method is used to make nano PAN polymers, and a chemical polymerization method is used to make nano polyaniline titanium dioxide quantum dots (PAN-TiQD) polymers as an active photocatalyst during the photodegradation of Dianix blue dye as an organic pollutant under xenon light. The traditional colloidal sol–gel method and the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method were used to make TiQD, which was then mixed with nanoPAN to make PAN-5 % TiQD (W/W) nanocomposites. Characterizations like XRD, FTIR, UV and visible diffuse reflectance spectra, TGA, surface area, EDX, and TEM were used to characterize the nanoPAN and PAN-TiQD that were made. Also, the photocatalytic activity of all synthesized polymers was investigated by the photodegradation process of Dianix blue dye as an organic hazardous pollutant in aqueous medium using a xenon photoreactor with an initial power of 75 W/Cm2. The effects of irradiation time and photocatalyst type on the photodegradation process efficiency of Dianix blue dye were examined. After 3 h of photodegradation processes, the photodegradation efficiency of PAN-TiQD reached 69 % compared to 36 % of nanoPAN due to the fact that it contains 5 % TiQD as a highly active catalyst, which reached 91 %. As an added value for this study, a significant contribution is the examination of eight hazardous industrial wastewater samples, which were assessed under direct sunlight and were in accordance with the KSA environmental permissible limit of 1000 ppm. Finally, the recycling processes in the presence of all synthesized catalysts were investigated and evaluated six times. The photodegradation rate of the recycling processes of Dianix blue dye was highly decreased in the presence of nano PAN than PAN-TiQD, while it had a low decreasing percentage in the case of TiQD. The recycling process using sunlight for industrial pollution samples was investigated at one of the KSA textile plants by using six synthetic catalysts and assessing COD and TOC values.

head.ce:title: Distinguishable photocatalytic activity of nano polyaniline with quantum dots metal oxide as photocatalysts for photodegradation of Dianix blue dye and different industrial pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanopolyaniline (PAN) polymers have recently been under active investigation as promising photocatalysts. In this work, a simple modified sol–gel method is used to make nano PAN polymers, and a chemical polymerization method is used to make nano polyaniline titanium dioxide quantum dots (PAN-TiQD) polymers as an active photocatalyst during the photodegradation of Dianix blue dye as an organic pollutant under xenon light. The traditional colloidal sol–gel method and the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method were used to make TiQD, which was then mixed with nanoPAN to make PAN-5 % TiQD (W/W) nanocomposites. Characterizations like XRD, FTIR, UV and visible diffuse reflectance spectra, TGA, surface area, EDX, and TEM were used to characterize the nanoPAN and PAN-TiQD that were made. Also, the photocatalytic activity of all synthesized polymers was investigated by the photodegradation process of Dianix blue dye as an organic hazardous pollutant in aqueous medium using a xenon photoreactor with an initial power of 75 W/Cm2. The effects of irradiation time and photocatalyst type on the photodegradation process efficiency of Dianix blue dye were examined. After 3 h of photodegradation processes, the photodegradation efficiency of PAN-TiQD reached 69 % compared to 36 % of nanoPAN due to the fact that it contains 5 % TiQD as a highly active catalyst, which reached 91 %. As an added value for this study, a significant contribution is the examination of eight hazardous industrial wastewater samples, which were assessed under direct sunlight and were in accordance with the KSA environmental permissible limit of 1000 ppm.

Finally, the recycling processes in the presence of all synthesized catalysts were investigated and evaluated six times. The photodegradation rate of the recycling processes of Dianix blue dye was highly decreased in the presence of nano PAN than PAN-TiQD, while it had a low decreasing percentage in the case of TiQD. The recycling process using sunlight for industrial pollution samples was investigated at one of the KSA textile plants by using six synthetic catalysts and assessing COD and TOC values.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Dianix blue dye and industrial pollutants

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nano polyaniline (PAN) is a type of conductive conjugated polymer that can have protonic acid in its structure. It is a popular material because it is easy and cheap to make, and it has excellent properties for electrical and optical applications. It also resists environmental degradation well [1–4]. The three forms of PAN have different electrical properties. Per-nigraniline, the completely oxidized form, is an insulator. Leucoemeraldine, the reduced form, and Emeraldine, the form halfway between the two, conduct electricity [2,5–7]. A powerful acid dopant, such as potassium peroxydisulfate (KPS), is often used during the oxidative aniline polymerization stage of conventional chemical polyaniline synthesis [6,8–12].

In recent years, PAN has seen increased use as a possible photocatalyst as a result of its distinctive photocatalytic activity [13–17] and photo-stability [17–19]. In photodegradation investigations, PAN serves as both a hole conductor and an electron donor. The photocatalytic activity of the newly synthesised photocatalysts may be improved by increasing both the electron-hole pair separation and the hole migration rate [19–21]. Photocatalysts are materials that can use light to trigger chemical reactions. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a special type of photocatalyst that has many advantages. It is stable, cheap, and can produce powerful oxidizing agents that can break down pollutants in water. This makes it a great choice for cleaning wastewater or groundwater [22–24] beside the interested applications in degrades organic hazardous materials [25]. Titania has three polymorphs: rutile, anatase and brookite. Anatase is known to possess the highest photoactivity compared to brookite or rutile because of the higher density of localized states, wider band gap, the larger surface area and slower charge-carrier recombination [26–30]. Titanium quantum dots (QDs) are man-made nanoparticles with a larger surface area than bulk titanium dioxide. This makes them more reactive and gives them a unique set of properties, including the quantum confinement effect. QDs are being explored for a variety of applications, including solar cells, bioimaging, and water purification. [31–34]. The chemical and physical properties of quantum dots are very different from their parent bulk, which refers to the effect of their small size and the extreme surface area-to-volume ratio. Consequently, researchers in the field of photocatalysis have recently demonstrated an increased interest in exploring their potential for various photocatalytic applications [35–39].

In a photocatalytic process, a photo-induced molecular transformation occurs at the catalyst surface. Also, if the photocatalyst is irradiated by light with a frequency greater than its band gap energy, electron-hole pairs are generated, leading to the formation of superoxide radical anions (O2–), hydroxyl radicals (–OH) and hydroperoxyl radicals (•OOH).In the photocatalytic process, these oxidizing radicals play a pivotal role in mediating oxidation reactions. Concurrently, electrons from the conduction band may be acquired by adsorbed dye molecules, culminating in anion formation. This interaction, however, can lead to the formation of dye radicals, which subsequently contribute to the degradation of the dye itself [40]. Recently, studies have shown that toxic and hazardous wastes from the dye industry and textiles have caused a certain environmental issue that needs many considerations. Many industrial organic dyes employed are non-biodegradable, toxic, and pose a significant threat to aquatic organisms when discharged into wastewater systems without prior treatment. [41–44]. Various photocatalysts can be employed to remove a wide range of dyes. [45–49]. Photocatalysis essentially depends on the formation of pairs of electrons and positive holes (e−/ h+), when the photocatalyst is irradiated by different light sources (UV and visible light).The electrons lead to the formation of superoxide anions in the presence of oxygen, while the positive holes react with water, leading to the formation of hydroxyl radicals, which are very strong oxidizers whose potential is 2.80 V, or oxidize the adsorbed organic substrates. The photocatalysts efficiency in water was experimentally determined with most of the organic pollutants, which reached about 1–2 g/L[50]. Several techniques, such as ion exchange, precipitation, chemical oxidation, filtration, coagulation, and adsorption, are used for the removal of dyes from industrial wastewater. [51,52]. Nearly 50 % of all the dyes used in industries are azoic dyes, which are characterized by (–NN–), where at least one of the N atoms is attached to an aromatic group (rings of benzene or naphthalene).Azo dyes have amphoteric characteristics due to the presence of other amino, hydroxyl, sulfonyl, or carboxyl functional groups. The compound can have cationic properties when the amino group is protonated, anionic properties when the acidic group is deprotonated, or non-ionic properties based on the pH of its surroundings. These characteristics classify it as a basic dye, acid dye, disperse dye, direct dye, sulfur dye, vat dye, or reactive dye. Dyes are categorized into anionic, non-ionic, and cationic types based on their inherent structural characteristics. [53–55].

In this work, and because of the TiQD wider band gap, which is reflected in the photocatalytic activity as compared with bulk and other usual nanostructured TiO2, TiQD was doped with PAN to increase the photochemical activities of PAN and used as a photocatalyst for the degradation of Dianix blue dye under direct sunlight and xenon irradiation sources.

Aniline 99.8 % pure, Potassium Persulphate (KPS), Polyvinylidene fluoride, hydrochloric acid (HCl) 37 %, and methanol were purchased from Acros Organics, N-Methy1-2-pyrrolidone was bought from Carlo Erba Reagents and used as received. Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TIPO, 97 %, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), deionized water (18.2MΩcm−1, Ultra-pure Milli-Q, France) and nitric acid (65 %, Panreac, Spain). All chemicals were of pure analytical grade and solutions were synthesized with deionized water.

The sol–gel method was used to synthesize polyaniline from aniline monomers, in accordance to methods as explained in references [56,57] with a few modifications. In one beaker aniline (20 mL) was dissolved in 250 mL HCl (0.12 M) at room temperature and in another beaker, 46 g of KPS was dissolved also in 250 mL of HCl (0.12 M). The KPS/HCl solution was added dropwise to the aniline/HCl mixture and manually mixed for half an hour, pH was recorded as 1.5. The produced precipitate was washed several times using distilled water, methanol, and HCl, then filtered and dried at 60 °C for 24 h in an electrical oven.

TiQDnanoparticles were synthesized combining conventional colloidal sol–gel with microwave-assisted hydrothermal method [58,59]. The synthesization process started as in any colloidal sol–gel process with the quick hydrolysis, under continuous stirring, of titanium (IV) isopropoxide in a mixture of deionized water and nitric acid in H2O:Ti4+ = 100:1 (500 mL H2O and 5 mL of titanium (IV) isopropoxide) and H+:Ti4+ = 0.2 M ratios. 25 mL of Nitric acid was used as a dispersing agent/catalyst. The solution was stirred continuously at 1000 rpm for 45 min, and then transferred to a 100-mL sealed vessel made of high-purity TFM (modified teflon) placed in a commercial microwave digestion system (Milestone ETHOS One, Italy) where hydrothermal reactions occurred at a microwave frequency of 3 GHz. The container was surrounded by a safety shield. Temperature and pressure during the synthesis were monitored and controlled with a shielded thermocouple directly inserted into the vessel and with a pressure transducer sensor connected to the vessel, respectively. Built-in magnetic stirring (Teflon-coated stirring bar) was used. The experimental method entailed developing a thermal gradient over 15 min and then sustaining it for 30 min at a maximum temperature of 210 °C. After cooling at room temperature, the obtained powders were carefully washed for at least three times with deionized water and centrifuged every time at a rotation speed of 3000 rpm using a multi-purpose bench top centrifuge (Núve NF800, Turkey).

PAN-TiQD composites were synthesized through in situ chemical polymerization. TiQD was taken as 5 % by weight of aniline hydrochloride monomer, suspended in the monomer solution using violent stirring for half an hour to achieve good distribution of TiQD in the solution. The polymerization process was carried out using the same method used to prepare polyaniline [60,61].

Nano PAN and PAN-TiQD were illustrated by means of Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) which was recorded using a PerkinElmer Frontier FT-IR Spectrometer, Germany; in chemical warfare administration laboratories, Powder XRD Philips differactrometer model X-Ṕert pro diffractometer, (Netherlands), where the patterns were run with Ni-filtered copper radiation (CuKα = 1.5404 °A at 10 mA and 30 kV) with a scanning speed of 2θ = 2.5°/min, Diffuse spectra, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) PerkinElmer\Pyris 1, Calibrations with starting analysis Temperature 30 °C to 900 °C, morphology structural of the products were examined by transmitting electron microscopy (TEM) thermos scientific talos F200i 200KV, (surface area) surface area &pore size analyzer N2 Nova3000e,Quantachrome instrument, USA N2, energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) microanalysis using SEM model (Quanta FEG250, EDAX, FEI-inspects model), apparatus model at 150 kV with magnifying an image at X 1.00 and X 100.000 by sending a beam of light through the object, and energy bandgap (EBG) techniques were used for characterization.

Xenon photoreactor (Eng. Co., Ltd.) with a water-cooling system to avoids the thermal xenon lamp effect during the photodegradation processes. The Xenon lamp power was 75 W/Cm2 and its wavelength ranged from 190 to 1090 nm. The experimental condition: adding 0.5 mg from the synthesized catalyst to 1 L of Dianix blue dye (4 × 10−5 M and, pH = 6.7). Then the synthesized catalysts were added to the dye with stirring for 20 min in the dark to confirm the adsorption/desorption equilibrium. The final step is to remove any remaining catalyst particles from the suspension by immediately centrifuging for 30 min at 12000 rpm using the Thermo MEGAFUGE 16 centrifuge. The photodegradation process rates of all synthesized samples spectrophotometrically determined as the following eq:

where C is the dye concentration at time t, C0 is the dye concentration at initial phase, t is photodegradation time in minute and kapp is the pseudo first order photodegradation rate constant. Also, direct sun light was used in the photodegradation processes, where its intensity was around 4.6 mW/cm2 for ultraviolet light and 1460 lx for visible light during the processes. The COD analysis for the photodegradation processes of the industrial pollutants from one factory in KSA was measured.

Also, under the same Dianix blue dye concentration (4 × 10−5 M and, pH = 6.7) with the same catalyst dosage, Na2EDTA was used as a scavenger for h+, p-benzoquinone BQ was used as a scavenger for . O2 –, isopropanol was used as a scavenger for ( . OH), and carbon tetrachloride CCl4 was used as a scavenger for e_ at a concentration of 1.0 mM.

Recycling processes for PAN-TiQD estimated under both xenon photoreactor for the Dianix blue dye and sun light for different industrial pollutants samples. The utilization of the synthesized PAN-TiQD was thoroughly cleaned with deionized water, centrifuged at a speed of 12,000 rpm, and then dried at 60 degrees Celsius for twenty-four hours. After drying, the same amount of the recovered photocatalyst was reused under the same experimental condition described above and also, the photodegradation process rates was determined. Total organic carbon (TOC) analysis used to reflect the pollutant mineralization degree during the photodegradation process via the TOC analyzer (Tekmar Dohrmann Phoenix 8000).

The XRD pattern of synthesized nano PAN, TiQD and PAN-TiQD are shown in (Fig. 1 ). Nano PAN doped with HCl becomes low crystalline or an amorphous structure at different diffraction peaks at 2θ = 16.1°, 21.9° and 24.9° according to JCPDS-ICDD file NO. 79-2497 which were identified as (011), (020) and (202) planes while the periodicity parallel to the polymer chain observed at 2θ = 21°and the periodicity perpendicular to the polymer chain observed 2θ = 25.6°. TiQD XRD was showing significantly highly crystallinity peaks at 25.6 and 37.9°, and other small peaks centered at 2θ = 48.7, 54.9, 62.2, 68.9 and 75.5° according to JCPDS-ICDD file NO. 84-1285 which were identified as (101), (004), (200), (105), (204), (301) and (215) planes.[62,63]. Peaks of XRD appeared in PA-TQD nanocomposites have indicating for TiQD presence. XRD spectra of nano PAN have many peaks at 2θ = 24.9° which assigned to periodicity perpendicular and parallel of the nano PAN chain direction as a result of nano PAN doped to TiO 2 composite. Also, nano PAN peak at 21° becomes less obvious but peak at 25.6° is overlapped with the TiO2 sharp crystalline peak, that result of increasing degree of crystallinity as compared to nano PAN and the above XRD results were supported by TEM measurement. XRD pattern proved TiQD presence in the nanocomposites synthesized [63–65].

Ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) conditions were used for the XPS experiments. The XPS data (Fig. 2 ) was acquired using the K-ALPHA (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) instrument using a monochromatic X-ray Al K-alpha radiation source with a −10 to 1350 eV energy range, 400 micro m spot size, 109 mbar pressure, 200 eV full spectrum pass energy, and 50 eV narrow spectrum. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to characterize the TiQD surface. The survey spectra showed evidence of the presence of carbon, oxygen, and titanium. Compared to the XP spectrum of TiO2 in the Ti 2p binding energy (BE) region (Fig. 2b), the C 1 s photoelectron peak at a binding energy (BE) of 286 eV was more prominent for TiQD (Fig. 2d). Ti3+ 2p3/2 at 454.37 eV, Ti3+ 2p1/2 at 459.51 eV, Ti4+ 2p3/2 at 460.60 eV, and Ti4+ 2p1/2 at 465.52 eV are the four components of the XPS Ti 2p peak. In particular, electron beam irradiation resulted in a decrease in Ti4+ concentrations and an increase in Ti3+ concentrations. It has been found that the amount of Ti3+ on the surface of TiQD doped with polymer atoms plays a significant influence. Ti3+ has the ability to trap photo-generated electrons, preventing them from mixing with minority carriers.

The XPS analysis of the O 1s spectra revealed three distinct peaks, corresponding to various oxygen-containing chemical bonds: the water molecule (HOH) at 531.85 eV, hydroxide (TiO-H) at 530.37 eV, and oxide (Ti-O-Ti) at 530.33 eV [66–69]. Notably, the peak for hydroxide, or bridging hydroxyls (TiOH), is attributed to the dissociative adsorption of water on oxygen vacancies, where the titanium ions connected to the hydroxyl group exhibit a +3 oxidation state. These Ti3+ ions function as Bronsted acid sites, anchoring water molecules to form a strongly bound OH-H2O complex [66–69] Consequently, the Ti3+ ions are oxidized to Ti4+ as the hydroxyl groups occupy the oxygen vacancies. Upon exposure to light, the photo-generated reducing electrons are readily transferred to water molecules at the active Ti-OH sites, facilitating proton reduction (Fig. 2c). The Ti 2p and O 1s peaks of titanium dioxide [70], as well as N 1s and C 1s, can be seen in the survey spectra of the TiQD particles (Fig. 2).

Fig. 3 (a and d) shows nano PAN TEM & HRTEM images, it can be noticed that the synthesized nano PAN nanoparticles have diameter ranging from 31 to 39 nm from the TEM image while Fig. 3 (b and e) shows TiQD TEM & HRTEM images where the thickness of the conformal TiO2 layer is around 3.0–4.9 nm that reveal TiQD was synthesized as quantum dots metal oxide. Also, Fig. 3 (c and f) shows PAN-TiQD TEM & HRTEM images, Consecutive junctions of TiQD and PAN are formed in the nanocomposite, PAN particles core encapsulated by TQD shell with 28–32 nm as an average total diameter which due to polymerization process of PAN nanoparticles surrounded by TiQD on PAN surface. Composite no distinct change was observed on the morphology of the composite except for the dispersion of titanium nanoparticles on the surface of PAN nanoparticle.

The effective properties of PAN-TiQD depend on the microstructural dispersion while the sol–gel method used for the preparation method of PAN-TiQD due to the miscibility control and proper dispersion of the prepared sample (PAN-TiQD). Also, according to the low temperature of the sol–gel method used in the preparation of our sample (PAN-TiQD), there are no structural defects observed. These observations are confirmed by (FTIR) absorption, where the peaks of the assigned wavelengths shifted to lower values, indicating good interaction between TiQD and PAN as show in Fig. 3.

The FTIR absorption spectra which considered as conformational analysis for all compounds of nano PAN, pure TiQD and PAN-TiQD nanocomposite were shown in Fig. 4 . The FTIR spectrum of nano PAN exhibits two peaks are observed at 1550 and 1469 cm−1 which are attributed to CN and CC asymmetric stretching vibrations of benzenoid and quinoid rings. Also, the peak at 1290 cm−1 is attributed to CN asymmetric stretching vibrations of secondary aromatic amine strengthened respectively by HCl doping and the peak at 1229 cm−1 is attributed to C–No+ –H asymmetric stretching vibration in the Polaron structure of nano PAN. While at 1119 cm−1 the observed peak is attributed to the (N+ H- stretching) and at 1039 cm−1 is C–Cl asymmetric vibration. At shorter wavenumber at 869 cm−1 C–H bond out-of-plane deformations vibration which was assigned to the existence of PAN in nanocomposite [64,65].

The FTIR of TiQD spectrum exhibits broad band appeared for Ti-O-Ti bonds at 670 cm−1 and for OH stretching vibration at 3420 cm −1
[33]
. FTIR of PAN-TiQD nanocomposite spectrum proves that the synthesized nano composite chemical structure is as suggested. The main characteristic band of nano composite are assigned as follows the absorption peak observed at 1559 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1 is attributed to CC and CN stretching modes for the quinoid NQN and benzenoid ring N–B–N while the band at 1299 cm−1 is attributed to the CN stretching vibration mode of secondary aromatic amine, the C– N O+ –H stretching vibration was observed at 1250 cm−1, the (NH) stretching vibration was shifted to 1130 cm−1 in the composite spectrum. The observed band at 799 cm−1 was assigned to aromatic CH out-of-plane deformation vibration. The band of TiQD (TiO2) of PAN-TiQD nanocomposite were observed at 709 cm−1 [63–65].

From diffuse reflectance spectra of the different synthesized samples, the relationship between the optical band gap energy (Eg) and the absorption coefficient (α) follows the formula: αhν = A (hν − Eg)1/2 (2)

where hν are the photon energy and A is a constant, respectively. The nano PAN band can be determined by extrapolating the relationship between (αhν)1/2 and hν. The nano PAN, TiQD and PAN-TiQD calculated band gaps are shown at Fig. 5 . The band gap decreases as the oxidation state increases, which is in compatible agreement with our previous results and these results also corresponds and supports the color change occurred in the nano PAN. Band gap obtained results of nano PAN, TiQD and PAN-TiQD are 2.4, 2.18 and 2.29 eV, respectively which have the same order of their size where it 31–39 nm for nano PAN, 3–4.9 nm for TiQD and 28–32 nm for PAN-TiQD respectively. The results obtained reflect that the composite which has TiQD increases the photocatalytic activity of the PAN-TiQD than nano PAN itself due to decrease in the size of PAN-TiQD than nano PAN. Also, the similar result was obtained from the surface area measurements of nano PAN, TiQD and PAN-TiQD which were 29.6 m2/g, 188.8 m2/g and 54.4 m2/g, respectively which matches band gap order observed where the presence of TiQD increases the particle size of the PAN-TiQD than nano PAN [14,71,72].

TGA curves of nano PAN synthesized where the residual wt. % refers to nano PAN weight percentage content as shown in Fig. 6 . Generally, nano PAN thermal behavior have a 3-step weight loss process indicating the majority weight loss for every step. The loss and escape of water molecules observed just below 100 °C as the first weight loss and the departure and degradation of acid dopant (HCl) observed ranging from 200 to 300 °C as the second weight loss, where the loss percent was about 14 %. The decomposition of PAN chains backbone starting at around 500 °C as the third weight loss and the loss percent reached about 29 %. After 500 °C, the polymers show a sharp weight loss, which can be correlated with the loss of the polymeric backbone and continued even after 800 °C the loss percent about 61 %. Finally, the intrinsic nano PAN total weight loss reaches 100 % in this study condition [64,73]. Also, TGA PAN-TiQD composite, which shows the stability of nano PAN after doped with TiQD as a composite where thermal stability was increased, the first weight loss at a temperature from 100 to 200 °C, was about 5 % compared to 9 % in polyaniline only. Second weight loss from a temperature of 200 to 900 °C degrees, the loss percentage is about 22 % compared to 66 % for pure polyaniline, which indicates an increase in thermal stability of PAN-TiQD. In the TGA of TiQD after 500oC, there is a slight increase in weight lean to stability towards heats up to a temperature of 900 °C, due to the nature of this element as an inorganic metal oxide either pyrolysis or calcinations [64,74,75].

The photocatalytic studies with Dianix blue dye follow first-order kinetics reactions and the correlation between photodegradation times, maximum photodegradation percentage, and rates of photodegradation of the synthesized samples demonstrated in Scheme 1 .

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a prominent material in photocatalysis, notable for its vast specific surface area, adaptable structure, excellent mobility of charge carriers at room temperature, superior thermal and electrical conductivities, and robust chemical stability. It also features a quantum-confined bandgap-narrowing mechanism, extending UV absorption into the visible spectrum. TiO2 quantum dots exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to bulk or nanosize TiO2 particles, primarily due to their quantum confinement effects and small size. These quantum dots, typically smaller than 10 nm, experience quantum effects altering their electronic band structure and increasing bandgap energy. This alteration allows for the absorption of a broader light spectrum, including visible light, thus significantly boosting photocatalytic efficiency under visible light [76–78]. Quantum dots have a higher surface area-to-volume ratio than nanosize TiO2 particles, offering more active sites for photocatalytic reactions and reducing charge carrier recombination. The high density of surface defects on quantum dots also acts as catalytic sites, further enhancing photocatalysis. While nanosize TiO2 particles may have a larger surface area compared to quantum dots, the latter’s ability to absorb visible light and their unique electronic properties due to quantum confinement give them an edge in photocatalytic applications. The crystal structure, whether anatase or rutile, also influences the photocatalytic performance of both quantum dots and nanosize particles [77–79].

Photodegradation percentages after irradiation by xenon lamp (75 Watt) for 3 h indicate that the photocatalytic for TiQD reached to (96 %) while PAN-TiQD recorded high photodegradation percentage (69 %) higher than nano PAN which recorded (36 %). This observation refers to effect of the photocatalytic activity of TiQD which increases the photodegradation rate constant of dye by nearly more than three times than in case of using PAN-TiQD (from 17.01 × 10-3 to 5.13 × 10-3 s−1) compared to nearly more than seven times than in nano PAN (from 17.01 × 10-3 to 2.34 × 10-3 s−1). Also, we depicted that the photodegradation rates of PAN-TiQD is more than double than that obtained in nano PAN (from 5.13.01 × 10-3 to 2.34 × 10-3 s−1).

The TOC results were obtained reflects the mineralization degree of Dianix blue Dye during the photocatalytic process till 180 min as shown in Fig. 7 (e). These results (62, 30 and 4 % for PAN, TiQD and PAN/TiQD, respectively) confirmed the values of the photodegradation % obtained from all photodegradation processes for the Dianix blue Dye (36, 69 and 96 % for PAN, TiQD and PAN/TiQD, respectively) because the relation between the mineralization % of Dianix blue Dye and the TOC % is inversely relation. Also, increasing as the photodegradation rate constants increases, which due to a decrease in the size of TiQDs because of the formation of the valance bands holes and the conduction bands electrons by radiation light (hυ) to the surface of the TiQDs prepared samples, where the doping ions inclusion in the TiQD crystal lattice can contribute to the formation of further holes traps and enormous electrons that bind with carriers, and suppress photocatalytic electrons and trousers which enhancing photocatalytic action. It appeared that the photocatalytic reaction performed of Dianix blue dye was affected by the quantum size of the TiQDs in both of TiQD and PAN/TiQD prepared samples where TiQD sample have high photocatalytic efficiency than that observed from PAN/TiQD as a result of the doping process occurred (Scheme 3 ).

Scavenger effect to investigate the most influential radical responsible for the photodegradation process was studied. This photocatalytic mechanism method is investigated by active species trapping experiments using various scavengers, as shown in Fig. 7 (f). In the absence of . O2, BQ and Na2EDTA showed a distinguishable decrease, indicating the effectiveness of . OH. After the illumination, the photogenerated electrons on the conduction band react with the surface oxygen molecules, creating . O2 – radicals that promote the Dianix blue dye degradation. While tapping with isopropanol and CCl4 reflected a distinguishable decrease that proposed the maximum effect of.OH and electrons [83,84]. The presence of Ti element that exhibits a high oxidation state (+4) can create some oxygen vacancies that might act as trapping centers for electrons, which in consequence, delay the e-/h + recombination, so the photogenerated holes could react with the surface adsorbed water molecules generating the effective . OH radicals [85]. Here, some of the general critical steps in the photocatalytic mechanism are investigated as follows [86–91]

From above bases, the hypothesized mechanism for the photodegradation of Dianix blue dye by TiQD starts in the valence band region. Electrons will be transported from the valence band area to the conduction band region when the catalyst is subjected to irradiation. This process starts in the area of the valence band and concludes in the conduction band region. One electron migrated to a higher level of excited negative electrons (e-), whereas a positive hole (h+) was generated at a lower level. Both of these things may be considered byproducts of the process.

Consequently, reactive species (superoxide and hydroxyl radicals) are seen when oxygen (O2) and water (H2O) molecules interact with e- and h+ to generate . O2 and . OH, respectively, considered to be reactive species, these radicals [91]. The mechanism of the photodegradation process is then fully activated when it targets the subsequence active components of the degrading Dianix blue dye e molecule.

In general, organic pollutant dyes cannot be broken entirely under sun light irradiation. According to the KSA Environmental Law [92,93], the permitted COD value is fewer than 1,000 ppm. In the realm of industrial waste management, a notable challenge arises from six specific hazardous wastewater samples procured from a dye-manufacturing facility. These samples contain a blend of organic dyes. The conventional decontamination processes prove inadequate for these samples, consequently leading to multifaceted health and environmental concerns. In addition, the organic impact on wastes is determined by calculating the quantity of oxygen needed for the conversion of organic wastes to CO2 and H2O (green products) using chemical oxygen demand analysis (COD). As shown in Fig. 8 , several parameters were investigated to determine the ideal COD levels for treating different industrial pollutants samples in the presence of nano PAN, TiQD and PAN-TiQD, respectively. The COD measured levels must be compared to the COD limit values permitted by KSA environmental legislation (1000 ppm).

The investigated industrial pollutants 8 samples had high COD values ranging from 5300 to 6900 (ppm), as shown in Fig. 8. Based on the presence and size effect of the TiQD, the obtained findings may be categorized and explained as follows: In the case of PAN nanoparticles, the COD values varied from (800 to 1380 ppm) and from (450 to 1050 ppm) for TiQD while in the case of PAN-TiQD, the COD values ranged from (680 to 1250 ppm). We determined that all COD levels for hazardous industrial wastewater samples exceed the maximum COD limit specified by KSA environmental legislation (COD must be less than 1000 ppm).

These results indicate that the photodegradation (mineralization) of industrial pollutants samples increased during the evaluation period of this study case due to an increase in photodegradation (photooxidation) processes rate in presence TiQD as an effective photocatalyst during the photodegradation process.

Fig. 9 shows that the rate of photodegradation processes reduces during the process of recycling in presence of all three synthesized samples till 6 times for across all photodegradation processes. The variation in the rates of photodegradation processes between all synthesized samples have distinguishable observation where the photodegradation rates decreases in presence of PAN nanoparticles, TiQD and PAN-TiQD by nearly about 53.5, 27.3 and 32.4 % respectively as shown in Fig. 10 . Also, it can be concluded that during the photodegradation processes using xenon irradiation source, the presence of TiQD in the recycling processes times have small effect on the photodegradation process due to the small decreasing in the photodegradation rates observed and let a chance for the synthesized catalyst to recycling for many times [10,94].

Recycling of the three synthesized photocatalysts is one of the distinct ecologically and economically required experiments for maintaining and reusing created catalysts during sunlight degradation and photodegradation processes. The reduced photodegradation rate in recycled photocatalysts is due to factors like byproduct accumulation, catalyst deactivation, loss of active sites, aging, catalyst aggregation, leaching, and contaminant buildup. Accumulation of reaction byproducts and contaminants hinders the catalyst's activity. Catalyst deactivation might occur from deposition of intermediates or changes in physical and chemical properties. Aging and wear from harsh conditions, UV exposure, and mechanical wear, along with catalyst aggregation and leaching of active materials, also contribute to decreased performance. To counter this, strategies like catalyst regeneration, surface modification, reaction condition optimization, and regular maintenance are employed to restore or enhance catalyst activity and maintain long-term effectiveness.

The recycling process effectiveness of the different three synthesized samples during the solar photocatalytic process for hazardous industrial wastewater treatment was tested at one of textile factories in, KSAby utilizing it 6 times and measuring the COD values. This recycling process is evaluated for four months (Jan. 2023 to April 2023) with different COD values according to the production lines in the dying factory (6900 ppm) for 9 h/day at morning period and of course, the starting COD value is dependent on the dyes used in the evaluation month, which affects the treatment rate and the number of repetitions required to meet the COD limit. Fig. 11 depicts this recycling assessment procedure after six repetitions of applying the different synthesized samples.

In this paper, new nanocomposites from nano PAN and TiQD were directly synthesized by using chemical oxidation polymerization method. The results of FTIR, EDX, and (UV–vis-DR) visible diffuse reflectance spectra measurements indicate that the nanocomposites containing both nano PAN and TiQD are successfully synthesized. Indeed, TEM images show TiQD particles core encapsulated by PAN shell with 31–39 nm as an average total diameter, XRD shows that, nano PAN becomes low crystalline or an amorphous structure, TiQD are crystalline and PAN-TiQD result, increasing degree of crystallinity as compared to nano PAN. TGA test indicate that increasing PAN stability to high temperature by adding TiQD in PAN-TiQD composite. Finally, the photodegradation rate in presence of PAN was increased by adding TiQD in PAN-TiQD by 120 % than its photodegradation rate in absence of TiQD. At the same study, via solar light and according to KSA environmental legislation (1000 ppm), The eight investigated hazardous industrial wastewater samples were evaluated. Also, 6 times of recycling processes were investigated in presence of all synthesized catalysts where the photodegradation rate decreases by nearly about 32.4, 27.3 and 53.4 % in presence of PA-TiQD, TiQD and nano PAN, respectively. While The recycling process via solar light for hazardous industrial wastewater samples was evaluated at one of dying factories in KSA by utilizing it 6 times via measuring the COD - TOC values. The empirical assessment of these materials in wastewater treatment, especially under direct sunlight, indicates their promise for sustainable, eco-friendly applications, albeit with diminishing efficacy upon multiple recycling cycles. The integration of such findings in industrial setups can pave the way for more sustainable wastewater management solutions.
